Chapter 10 Trigonometric Functions 10.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Graph the six trigonometric functions. Identify amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift of a sinusoidal function. Evaluate inverse trigonometric functions. 10.2 Graphs of Trig Functions The graphs of \(\sin(\theta)\) and \(\cos(\theta)\) are smooth, repeating waves — direct pictures of the unit-circle coordinates from Chapter 8, plotted as \(\theta\) sweeps from \(0\) to \(2\pi\) and beyond. Both have the same basic wave shape; \(\cos(\theta)\) is simply \(\sin(\theta)\) shifted left by \(\pi/2\). Figure 10.1: Graphs of sine and cosine over two full periods. Every point where the curve crosses zero, reaches its peak, or reaches its trough tells you something about the cycle — and that vocabulary (amplitude, period, phase shift, vertical shift) is exactly what lets us fit these curves to real cyclical data. 10.3 Sinusoidal Functions A general sinusoidal function is written: \[ f(t) = A\sin\big(B(t-C)\big) + D \] Amplitude (\(A\)): half the distance between the peak and trough — how far the curve swings above and below its midline. Period (\(2\pi/B\)): how long one complete cycle takes. Phase shift (\(C\)): how far the curve is shifted horizontally. Vertical shift (\(D\)): the midline the curve oscillates around. 10.3.1 Worked Example: Modeling Daylight Hours Suppose daylight hours in Seattle over the course of a year are approximately: \[ H(t) = 3.5\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 80)\right) + 12.2 \] where \(t\) is day of the year (\(t=1\) is Jan 1) and \(H(t)\) is hours of daylight. Step 1 — Read off each parameter. \(A = 3.5\), \(B = \dfrac{2\pi}{365}\), \(C = 80\), \(D = 12.2\). Step 2 — Interpret each one. Amplitude \(3.5\): daylight swings 3.5 hours above and below the midline. Period \(= \dfrac{2\pi}{2\pi/365} = 365\) days — one full cycle per year, as expected. Phase shift \(80\): the curve is shifted so that day 80 (roughly the spring equinox) is where daylight is at the midline and increasing — a reasonable match for when day length crosses its average value on the way up. Vertical shift \(12.2\): the average day length across the year is 12.2 hours. Step 3 — Predict the longest day. The maximum of \(\sin\) is 1, so the longest day has \(H = 3.5(1)+12.2 = 15.7\) hours. Figure 10.2: Modeled daylight hours across a calendar year. Concept Check: Identifying Amplitude/Period/Shift 1. For \(f(t) = 4\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) + 10\), identify the amplitude, period, and vertical shift. Click to reveal answer Amplitude \(=4\). Period \(= \dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/6} = 12\). Vertical shift \(=10\) (no phase shift, since there’s no \((t-C)\) term). 2. If a tidal height model has amplitude 1.2 m and vertical shift 3 m, what are the highest and lowest tide heights it predicts? Click to reveal answer Highest: \(3+1.2 = 4.2\) m. Lowest: \(3-1.2 = 1.8\) m. 10.4 Inverse Trigonometric Functions Just as with the general inverses from Chapter 5, an inverse trig function undoes a trig function — but with a caveat: since sine, cosine, and tangent are not one-to-one (they repeat forever), their inverses are only defined by restricting to one cycle’s worth of output. \(\sin^{-1}(x)\) (also written \(\arcsin(x)\)) returns an angle between \(-\pi/2\) and \(\pi/2\); \(\cos^{-1}(x)\) returns an angle between \(0\) and \(\pi\). 10.4.1 Worked Example: Solving for Time-of-Year Using the daylight model \(H(t) = 3.5\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80)\right)+12.2\), find a day of the year when daylight is exactly 14 hours. Step 1 — Isolate the sine term. \[ 14 - 12.2 = 3.5\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80)\right) \Rightarrow \sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80)\right) = \frac{1.8}{3.5} \approx 0.514 \] Step 2 — Apply the inverse sine. \[ \frac{2\pi}{365}(t-80) = \sin^{-1}(0.514) \approx 0.539 \text{ radians} \] Step 3 — Solve for \(t\). \[ t - 80 = 0.539 \times \frac{365}{2\pi} \approx 31.3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t \approx 111.3 \] Step 4 — Interpret. Day 111 of the year (roughly late April) is predicted to have 14 hours of daylight. (Because sine repeats, there’s a second day later in the year with the same daylight total, on the way back down — inverse sine only hands you one of the two solutions directly.) 10.5 Practice: Fit a Sinusoidal Model to Seasonal Data 10.5.1 What Are You Practicing? Reading amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift off a sinusoidal equation or graph, and using inverse trig to solve for an unknown input. 10.5.2 How to Approach These Problems Match the given equation to the general form \(A\sin(B(t-C))+D\) term by term. Compute period as \(2\pi/B\) if you need it. To solve for an unknown \(t\), isolate the sine or cosine term first, then apply the inverse function, then finish solving algebraically. 10.5.3 Practice Problems 1. Tidal height at a harbor is modeled by \(y(t) = 1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) + 2.5\), \(t\) in hours after midnight. Find the amplitude, period, and predicted high and low tide heights. Click to reveal solution Amplitude \(=1.5\). Period \(=\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/6}=12\) hours. High tide: \(2.5+1.5=4.0\) m. Low tide: \(2.5-1.5=1.0\) m. 2. Using the same tidal model, find a time \(t\) (between 0 and 6) when the tide height is exactly 3.5 m. Click to reveal solution \(3.5-2.5 = 1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) \Rightarrow \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) = \dfrac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.667\). Then \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t = \sin^{-1}(0.667)\approx 0.730\), so \(t \approx 0.730\times\dfrac{6}{\pi}\approx 1.39\) hours after midnight. 10.6 Skills Drills 10.6.1 Graphing Transformed Sinusoids For each function, state the amplitude, period, and vertical shift. \(f(t) = 2\sin(t) + 5\) \(f(t) = 6\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}t\right)\) \(f(t) = 0.5\sin(2t) - 3\) \(f(t) = 10\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}t\right) + 20\) Click to reveal answers Amplitude 2, period \(2\pi\), vertical shift 5 Amplitude 6, period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/4}=8\), vertical shift 0 Amplitude 0.5, period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{2}=\pi\), vertical shift \(-3\) Amplitude 10, period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/12}=24\), vertical shift 20 10.6.2 Evaluating Inverse Trig Functions Evaluate each (in radians). \(\sin^{-1}(1)\) \(\cos^{-1}(0)\) \(\sin^{-1}(0.5)\) \(\cos^{-1}(1)\) Click to reveal answers \(\pi/2\) \(\pi/2\) \(\pi/6\) \(0\) Test 4 Preview This chapter is the second of three chapters covered on Test 4. See the Test 4 Review chapter in the back matter once Chapter 10 is complete.

Chapter 11 Sinusoidal Equations & Modeling 11.1 Learning Objectives 11.2 Solving Sinusoidal Equations 11.3 The Full Modeling Workflow 11.4 Capstone Case Study 11.5 Practice: Capstone Modeling Project 11.6 Skills Drills: Mixed Review Test 4 Preview

Chapter 11 Sinusoidal Equations & Modeling 11.1 Learning Objectives By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Solve sinusoidal equations for specific input values. Carry out the full modeling workflow: data -> equation -> prediction. Synthesize precalculus tools across the course in a capstone project. 11.2 Solving Sinusoidal Equations Solving a sinusoidal equation for a specific input follows the same pattern as the inverse-trig worked example in Chapter 9: isolate the sine (or cosine) term, apply the inverse function, then finish the algebra. The one new wrinkle worth naming explicitly here: because sine and cosine repeat every cycle, a sinusoidal equation almost always has more than one valid solution within a given time span, even though the inverse function only ever returns one of them directly. 11.2.1 Worked Example: Two Solutions, One Cycle Using the harbor tide model from Chapter 9, \(y(t) = 1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right)+2.5\), find all times \(t\) between 0 and 12 hours when the tide is at 3.5 m. Step 1 — Isolate and apply the inverse, as before. \[ \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) = \frac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.667 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\pi}{6}t = \sin^{-1}(0.667) \approx 0.730 \] \[ t \approx 0.730 \times \frac{6}{\pi} \approx 1.39 \text{ hours} \] This matches the single solution found in Chapter 9 — but it isn’t the only time in the 12-hour window when the tide hits 3.5 m. Step 2 — Find the second solution using symmetry. Sine is symmetric about its peak: if \(\theta_0\) is one solution to \(\sin(\theta) = k\), then \(\pi - \theta_0\) is the other solution within one cycle. So: \[ \frac{\pi}{6}t = \pi - 0.730 \approx 2.412 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t \approx 2.412\times\frac{6}{\pi}\approx 4.61 \text{ hours} \] Step 3 — Interpret. The tide reaches 3.5 m twice in this 12-hour cycle: once around \(t\approx1.39\) hours (rising toward high tide) and again around \(t\approx4.61\) hours (falling back down from high tide). Reporting only the first answer from the inverse sine would have missed half the picture. Why Two Solutions? Sketch (or picture) the tide curve from the example above. Mark the horizontal line \(y=3.5\). How many times does the curve cross that line between \(t=0\) and \(t=12\)? Does that match the two solutions found above? Concept Check: Solving for a Specific Time 1. For \(y(t) = 4\sin(t)\), one solution to \(y(t)=2\) is \(t = \sin^{-1}(0.5) = \pi/6\). What is the second solution within \(0\) to \(2\pi\)? Click to reveal answer Using the symmetry rule: \(t = \pi - \pi/6 = 5\pi/6\). 11.3 The Full Modeling Workflow Every chapter in this book has built a piece of the same workflow: take a real quantity, decide what family of function best matches how it behaves, fit that function’s parameters to data, and use the resulting equation to predict, interpret, or solve. This section walks through that workflow start to finish, on a single dataset. 11.3.1 Worked Example: From Data to Prediction Suppose a research buoy records water temperature at the surface of a lake every month for a year, and the values trace out a clear seasonal cycle: a summer peak around 22°C and a winter low around 6°C, with the peak occurring near the start of August (month 8, if January is month 1). Step 1 — Choose the function family. A repeating annual cycle is a strong signal for a sinusoidal function (Chapter 9), not linear, exponential, or rational — this is the “shape of change” recognition skill from the very first chapter of this book, now applied to real data. Step 2 — Extract amplitude and vertical shift from the peak and trough. \[ D = \frac{22+6}{2} = 14, \qquad A = \frac{22-6}{2} = 8 \] Step 3 — Set the period. One full annual cycle: period \(=12\) months, so \(B = \dfrac{2\pi}{12} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\). Step 4 — Set the phase shift using the peak location. Sine reaches its maximum a quarter-cycle (3 months) after it crosses the midline going up. Since the peak is at month 8, the midline-crossing (going up) is at month 5, so \(C = 5\). Step 5 — Assemble the model. \[ T(t) = 8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-5)\right) + 14 \] Step 6 — Use it to predict. What temperature does the model predict for month 11 (November)? \[ T(11) = 8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(11-5)\right)+14 = 8\sin(\pi)+14 = 8(0)+14 = 14°C \] Figure 11.1: A fitted sinusoidal model of lake surface temperature across a year, with the November prediction marked. 11.4 Capstone Case Study Putting It All Together A watershed manager wants to predict the seasonal peak concentration of a nutrient that spikes each spring due to snowmelt runoff, then decays over the summer as flows subside. Using tools from across this book, sketch — in words — how you would approach this: What shape of function would you use for the spring spike and rise? (Chapter 4 or 9?) What shape of function would you use for the summer decay? (Chapter 6?) What would you need to know about the data to fit the parameters of your chosen function(s)? 11.5 Practice: Capstone Modeling Project 11.5.1 What You’re Practicing Choosing the right function family from the whole course based on a system’s described behavior, fitting parameters from given features (peak, trough, timing, rate), and using the resulting model to predict or solve. 11.5.2 Practice Problems 1. A river’s turbidity (cloudiness) rises sharply after each rainstorm and then decays back toward a clear baseline over the following days, in a pattern that repeats after every storm but doesn’t cycle on a fixed calendar schedule. Which function family from this book best matches a single post-storm decay — exponential, sinusoidal, or quadratic? Explain your reasoning in one sentence. Click to reveal solution Exponential. A single storm’s turbidity spike decaying back toward a baseline is a decay-toward-equilibrium pattern (like the lake temperature after a cold snap in Chapter 6) — it isn’t a repeating annual cycle (which would call for sinusoidal), and it isn’t described by a single symmetric peak the way a quadratic optimization problem is. 2. A coastal air temperature cycles annually with a high of 26°C in July (month 7) and a low of 10°C in January (month 1). Build a sinusoidal model \(T(t)\) for this cycle, and predict the temperature in April (month 4). Click to reveal solution \(D = \dfrac{26+10}{2}=18\), \(A=\dfrac{26-10}{2}=8\), period 12 months so \(B=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\). Peak at month 7 means midline-crossing (rising) at month 4, so \(C=4\): \[ T(t) = 8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-4)\right)+18 \] At \(t=4\): \(T(4) = 8\sin(0)+18 = 18°C\) — which makes sense, since month 4 was defined as the midline-crossing point itself. 11.6 Skills Drills: Mixed Review A short mixed set, spanning tools from across the whole course. Convert 2.5 metric tons to kg. (Chapter 1) Find the intersection of \(y=2x+3\) and \(y=-x+9\). (Chapter 2) Evaluate the piecewise function \(f(x) = \begin{cases}x^2 & x<0\\2x & x\ge0\end{cases}\) at \(x=-3\) and \(x=3\). (Chapter 3) Find the vertex of \(f(x) = -x^2+6x\). (Chapter 4) Given \(f(x)=x+1\), \(g(x)=3x\), find \((g\circ f)(2)\). (Chapter 5) Solve \(4^x = 64\). (Chapter 6) Convert \(120°\) to radians. (Chapter 7) Evaluate \(\sin(\pi/3)\) using the unit circle. (Chapter 8) Find the period of \(f(t) = 5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}t\right)\). (Chapter 9) Click to reveal answers \(2500\) kg \(2x+3=-x+9 \Rightarrow x=2, y=7\): \((2,7)\) \(f(-3)=9\), \(f(3)=6\) \(x=3\), \(f(3) = -(3)^2+6(3) = -9+18 = 9\): vertex \((3,9)\) \(f(2)=3\), \(g(3)=9\) \(x=3\) \(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\) \(\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\) \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/4}=8\) Test 4 Preview This chapter concludes the material covered on Test 4 (Chapters 8–10) — the last test of the course. There is no cumulative final exam.

Chapter 12 How to Use This Workbook

Chapter 12 How to Use This Workbook Each workbook chapter mirrors the content chapter of the same name, and is meant to be worked through in class, together. Every chapter follows the same three-part structure: Instructor Demo. A worked example the instructor walks through live, modeling the thinking process out loud — not just the algebra. If you’re using this as a self-study resource outside of class, work through the demo yourself before moving on, the same way you would in a class session. Your Turn. A short set of problems for you to attempt on your own or in a small group, right after each demo, while the ideas are fresh. These are similar in difficulty to the demo problems. Challenge. Every workbook chapter ends with a Challenge section, split into two parts: Harder Versions — problems that extend the same skill further than the main Your Turn problems do. Environmental & Biological Applications — problems that apply the chapter’s tools to new environmental or biological contexts, for students who want an extra stretch. The Challenge section is optional extension material — it’s there for students who finish early or want more practice, not a required baseline. If you’re moving quickly through the main material, spend more time here; if the main material is still settling, it’s completely fine to skip Challenge problems for now and return to them later. Answer keys for the Your Turn and Challenge problems are collected at the end of each workbook chapter, click-to-reveal. Instructor Demo problems are intentionally left unworked in this text — the instructor works through them live. Suggested Pacing Each workbook chapter is designed for a class session structured as: ~20 minutes — mini-lecture / content introduction (using the matching content chapter) ~60 minutes — this workbook: Instructor Demos, Your Turn practice, and (time permitting) Challenge problems Within the 60-minute workbook block, a reasonable pace is a short live demo (5–10 minutes) followed by Your Turn problems for that same topic before moving to the next topic. Groups that move quickly should head into the Challenge section rather than waiting; groups that need more time on the core Your Turn problems should treat Challenge as optional and skippable.

Notes: 13.2 Unit Conversion 13.2.1 Instructor Demo: A Pesticide Application Rate A farm applies pesticide at a rate of 3.5 lb per acre. Regulators want this expressed in kg per hectare (1 lb ≈ 0.4536 kg, 1 acre ≈ 0.4047 hectares). (Instructor walks through this live: identify the given and target units, list conversion facts, chain the factors, check reasonableness.) 13.2.2 Your Turn: Unit Conversion Practice 1. A stormwater outfall discharges at 450 gallons per minute. Convert this to liters per second. (1 gallon ≈ 3.785 L) 2. A soil sample contains 12 mg of lead per kg of soil. Express this as parts per million (ppm), given that for solid samples, mg/kg is exactly equivalent to ppm by mass. 3. A vehicle fleet’s emissions are reported as 0.8 lb of NOx per mile. Convert this to grams per kilometer (1 lb ≈ 453.6 g, 1 mile ≈ 1.609 km). 4. A reservoir loses water to evaporation at 2.2 acre-feet per day. Given that 1 acre-foot ≈ 1,233,000 L, express this loss rate in liters per hour. 5. A composting facility processes 3 metric tons of food waste per day. Express this rate in kg per hour. 6. A well’s water table drops 8 inches over 6 months. Convert this to centimeters per year (1 inch = 2.54 cm). Notes: 13.3 Coordinate Systems & The Distance Formula 13.3.1 Instructor Demo: Locating a New Sampling Site A field crew sets up a local grid centered on a reference stake. Station P sits at \((45, -20)\) and Station Q sits at \((-30, 60)\), in meters east/north of the stake. (Instructor walks through this live: plot both points, identify quadrants, then apply the distance formula step by step to find the distance between P and Q.) 13.3.2 Your Turn: Coordinates & Distance 1. Plot the points \((20, 15)\), \((-10, 5)\), and \((0, -25)\) on a coordinate grid. Which quadrant (or axis) does each fall on? 2. Two soil probes are located at \((15, 40)\) and \((85, -20)\), in meters. Find the straight-line distance between them. 3. A third probe is placed exactly halfway between the two probes in Question 2 (using the midpoint of each coordinate). What are its coordinates? (Hint: average the x-values, then average the y-values.) 4. A weather station sits at \((-45, 30)\) and a rain gauge sits at \((15, -10)\), in meters. Find the distance between them. 5. Plot four points: \((10,10)\), \((10,-10)\), \((-10,-10)\), \((-10,10)\). What shape do they form, and what is its perimeter? (Use the distance formula for each side, even though you could find the side length by inspection — practice the formula.) 6. A drone launch point is at \((0,0)\). It needs to reach a wildlife camera at \((150, 200)\) meters away. How far must it fly in a straight line? Notes: 13.4 Challenge 13.4.1 Harder Versions 1. Three monitoring stations sit at \((0,0)\), \((60, 0)\), and \((30, 52)\). Find the perimeter of the triangle they form by computing all three pairwise distances and summing them. Notes: 13.4.2 Environmental & Biological Applications 1. A predator’s home range is roughly circular with a 3 km radius, centered on a den site at \((0,0)\) on a local grid. Justify your answer using the distance formula (recall the circle/buffer-zone reasoning from the content chapter). 2. A biologist studying bioaccumulation notes that a contaminant present at 0.002 mg/L in lake water is found at a concentration in fish tissue that is 100,000 times higher on a per-kg basis. Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Unit Conversion — Your Turn \(450\ \tfrac{\text{gal}}{\text{min}} \times \dfrac{3.785\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{gal}} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{min}}{60\ \text{s}} \approx 28.4\ \text{L/s}\) \(12\) ppm (mg/kg converts directly to ppm by mass for a solid sample) \(0.8\ \tfrac{\text{lb}}{\text{mi}} \times \dfrac{453.6\ \text{g}}{1\ \text{lb}} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{mi}}{1.609\ \text{km}} \approx 225.5\ \text{g/km}\) \(2.2\ \tfrac{\text{acre-ft}}{\text{day}} \times \dfrac{1{,}233{,}000\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{acre-ft}} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{day}}{24\ \text{hr}} \approx 113{,}025\ \text{L/hr}\) \(3\ \text{tons/day} \times \dfrac{1000\ \text{kg}}{1\ \text{ton}} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{day}}{24\ \text{hr}} = 125\ \text{kg/hr}\) \(8\ \text{in} \times \dfrac{2.54\ \text{cm}}{1\ \text{in}} = 20.32\ \text{cm over 6 months}\); annualized: \(20.32 \times 2 = 40.64\ \text{cm/yr}\) Coordinates & Distance — Your Turn \((20,15)\): Quadrant I. \((-10,5)\): Quadrant II. \((0,-25)\): on the y-axis. \(d = \sqrt{(85-15)^2+(-20-40)^2} = \sqrt{70^2+(-60)^2} = \sqrt{4900+3600}=\sqrt{8500}\approx 92.2\) m Midpoint: \(\left(\dfrac{15+85}{2}, \dfrac{40+(-20)}{2}\right) = (50, 10)\) \(d = \sqrt{(15-(-45))^2+(-10-30)^2} = \sqrt{60^2+(-40)^2} = \sqrt{3600+1600}=\sqrt{5200}\approx72.1\) m A square with side length 20; perimeter \(=4\times20=80\) (each side confirmed via the distance formula, e.g. \(\sqrt{(10-10)^2+(10-(-10))^2}=20\)) \(d = \sqrt{150^2+200^2} = \sqrt{22500+40000}=\sqrt{62500}=250\) m Challenge — Harder Versions \(800\ \text{lb} \times 0.025 = 20\ \text{lb N}\). Convert to kg: \(20 \times 0.4536 \approx 9.07\) kg N per acre. Convert per-acre to per-hectare: \(9.07\ \tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{acre}} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{acre}}{0.4047\ \text{ha}} \approx 22.4\ \text{kg N/ha}\). Distances: \((0,0)\)–\((60,0)\): \(60\). \((60,0)\)–\((30,52)\): \(\sqrt{30^2+52^2}=\sqrt{900+2704}=\sqrt{3604}\approx60.0\). \((30,52)\)–\((0,0)\): \(\sqrt{30^2+52^2}\approx60.0\). Perimeter \(\approx 60+60.0+60.0 = 180.0\). Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications Distance from origin: \(\sqrt{4.5^2+2^2} = \sqrt{20.25+4}=\sqrt{24.25}\approx4.92\) km. Since \(4.92 > 3\), the second den is outside the first predator’s 3 km home range. \(0.002\ \text{mg/L} \times 100{,}000 = 200\ \text{mg/kg} = 200\ \text{ppm}\) in fish tissue.

A fish population in a hatchery-supplemented stream is modeled by \(P(t) = 40t + 500\) (with supplementation) and, if supplementation stopped, would instead follow \(P(t) = -15t + 500\), where \(t\) is years. The well’s water level is rising (depth below surface decreasing) by 0.75 m/year. \(C(x) = 0.12x + 15\) The concentration is decreasing by 2 mg/L per year. Zero when \(0 = -2x+40 \Rightarrow x=20\) years. Slope \(=\dfrac{1-9}{6-2}=-2\); \(y-9=-2(x-2) \Rightarrow y=-2x+13\) \(y = 15x\); at \(x=12\): \(y=180\) m \(C_1\) has the higher starting cost ($2000 vs $500); \(C_2\) has the higher per-acre cost ($650 vs $500) Circles & Buffer Zones — Your Turn \((x-10)^2+(y+5)^2=3600\) Center \((20,-10)\), radius \(50\) \(\sqrt{70^2+80^2}=\sqrt{4900+6400}=\sqrt{11300}\approx106.3\), outside the 100 m buffer \(\sqrt{100^2+50^2}=\sqrt{10000+2500}=\sqrt{12500}\approx111.8 < 150\), inside the zone Radius \(=\sqrt{0^2+15^2}=15\); equation: \((x+15)^2+(y-25)^2=225\) Distance between centers \(=120\); sum of radii \(=80+60=140\). Since \(120<140\), the circles overlap. Modeling, Intersections & Motion — Your Turn \(3x+2=-x+18 \Rightarrow x=4, y=14\): \((4,14)\) \(25x+300=40x+150 \Rightarrow 15x=150 \Rightarrow x=10\) years \(x(t)=8t\), \(y(t)=50-3t\); at \(t=5\): \((40, 35)\) \(x(t)=-200+12t\), \(y(t)=4t\); at \(t=10\): \((-80, 40)\) \(10t = 100-5t \Rightarrow 15t=100 \Rightarrow t=6.67\) hr. y-coordinates: \(y_A=6(6.67)=40\), \(y_B=6(6.67)=40\) — yes, they match (both move at the same \(v_y=6\), so if x matches, y automatically matches too). \(5+2x = 12-0.5x \Rightarrow 2.5x=7 \Rightarrow x=2.8\) years Challenge — Harder Versions Substituting \(y=x\): \(x^2+x^2=r^2 \Rightarrow 2x^2=r^2 \Rightarrow x=\pm\dfrac{r}{\sqrt2}\). Crossing points: \(\left(\dfrac{r}{\sqrt2},\dfrac{r}{\sqrt2}\right)\) and \(\left(-\dfrac{r}{\sqrt2},-\dfrac{r}{\sqrt2}\right)\). Line 1 & 2: \(2x+5=-x+20 \Rightarrow x=5,y=15\). Line 1 & 3: \(2x+5=0.5x+10 \Rightarrow x=3.33,y=11.67\). Line 2 & 3: \(-x+20=0.5x+10\Rightarrow x=6.67,y=13.33\). Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Functions, Domain & Range — Your Turn Yes — each date has exactly one ozone reading. Depth is a function of distance only if each distance has one depth (it does, per the setup); distance is not a function of depth, since one depth value corresponds to two different distances (where the lakebed dips and rises back to the same depth). Domain: \(3 \le v \le 25\). Range: \(P(3)=45\) to \(P(25)=375\), so \(45 \le P \le 375\) kW. Domain \(10\le T\le35\). \(A(10)=5\), \(A(35)=55\): range \(5\le A\le55\). \(f(0)=1\); \(f(3)=18-15+1=4\); \(f(-2)=8+10+1=19\) Domain: \(5000-200t\ge0 \Rightarrow t\le25\), so \(0\le t\le25\). At \(t=25\), storage reaches exactly 0 — the reservoir is fully drawn down. Piecewise Functions — Your Turn \(C(x)=\begin{cases}1.50x & 0\le x\le20\\30+2.75(x-20) & x>20\end{cases}\). \(C(30)=30+2.75(10)=57.50\) \(C(15)=1.50(15)=22.50\) \(P(x)=\begin{cases}0 & 0\le x\le5\\25(x-5) & 5<x\le50\\1125+40(x-50) & x>50\end{cases}\) \(P(70)=1125+40(20)=1925\) \(E(a)=\begin{cases}5 & a<12\\12 & a\ge12\end{cases}\). Not continuous — just below 12 the value is 5, and at 12 it jumps to 12 (a real jump, unlike the water-rate example in the content chapter). \(C(0.5)=20(0.5)=10\). \(C(4)=-4(3)+20=8\) Challenge — Harder Versions Fee for tons 100–500 (400 tons): \(400r\). Fee for tons above 500 (200 tons): \(200(2r)=400r\). Total: \(400r+400r=800r=24000 \Rightarrow r=30\). Continuity at \(x=2\): \(f(2^-) = 2^2=4\), so \(m(2)+b=4\). Also \(f(4)=4m+b=10\). Subtracting: \((4m+b)-(2m+b)=10-4 \Rightarrow 2m=6 \Rightarrow m=3\). Then \(2(3)+b=4 \Rightarrow b=-2\). Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications Domain: \(5\le T\le40\). Range: \(M(5)=35\) to \(M(40)=140\), so \(35\le M\le140\). Unlike a purely abstract function, restricting the domain here reflects a real biological limit — the formula might produce a number outside \(5\)–\(40°C\), but that number wouldn’t correspond to a living organism, so it’s not just a math convenience, it’s a statement about survivability. \(R(x)=\begin{cases}0 & 0\le x\le50\\8(x-50) & 50<x\le200\\1200+15(x-200) & x>200\end{cases}\). \(R(250)=1200+15(50)=1950\)

At 10 years post-restoration, population peaks at 150. Vertex \((3,-8)\), opens upward (\(a=2>0\)); y-intercept: \(f(0)=2(9)-8=10\) Optimization Problems — Your Turn \(2x+2y=400 \Rightarrow y=200-x\); \(A(x)=x(200-x)=-x^2+200x\); vertex at \(x=100\), so \(y=100\): a 100 m × 100 m square. Vertex: \(t=-\dfrac{30}{2(-5)}=3\); \(h(3)=-45+90=45\) m at \(t=3\) s. Perimeter budget: \(9000/45=200\) m; same setup: \(x=y=50\), a 50 m × 50 m square. Vertex: \(x=-\dfrac{2}{2(-0.02)}=50\); \(h(50)=-0.02(2500)+100=50\) m at \(x=50\) m. Vertex: \(x=-\dfrac{80}{2(-2)}=20\); \(R(20)=-800+1600=800\); release 20,000 juveniles for a maximum predicted return of 800 (thousand, consistent with the model’s units). Vertex: \(x=-\dfrac{-40}{2(0.5)}=40\); \(C(40)=0.5(1600)-1600+1200=800-1600+1200=400\); minimum cost of 400 at a treatment rate of 40 (thousand gal/day). Challenge — Harder Versions Let \(x\) = the two widths, \(y\) = the one length: \(2x+y=240 \Rightarrow y=240-2x\). \(A(x)=x(240-2x)=-2x^2+240x\). Vertex: \(x=-\dfrac{240}{2(-2)}=60\); \(y=240-120=120\). Dimensions: 60 m (each width) × 120 m (length), enclosing \(60\times120=7200\) m². Vertex form: \(f(x)=a(x-20)^2+500\). Using \((0,900)\): \(900=a(400)+500 \Rightarrow 400a=400 \Rightarrow a=1\). So \(f(x)=(x-20)^2+500 = x^2-40x+400+500=x^2-40x+900\). Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications Vertex: \(x=-\dfrac{120}{2(-4)}=15\); prey maximized at 15 predators. Beyond 15 predators, the model predicts prey decline — ecologically, more predators eventually means more predation pressure than the prey population can sustain, overwhelming whatever earlier benefit (e.g., controlling a different competing pressure) the model assumes at lower predator densities. Vertex: \(x=-\dfrac{24}{2(-0.8)}=15\); \(E(15)=-0.8(225)+360=-180+360=180\)… this exceeds 100%, which is a reminder that this simplified model shouldn’t be trusted far from realistic plant densities — but within the model, plant density of 15/m² maximizes efficiency. Both very low density (too little filtration surface) and very high density (likely overcrowding, competition for light/nutrients, or flow blockage in a real wetland) reduce efficiency below this peak, which is exactly the downward-opening parabola shape.

Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Composing Functions — Your Turn \(f(2)=7\), \(g(7)=21\) \(g(2)=6\), \(f(6)=11\) \(f(3)=5\), \(g(5)=25\) \((f\circ g)(x) = 2x^2-1\) \(R(C(s)) = 10(0.2s+1)-3 = 2s+10-3 = 2s+7\) \(2(20)+7=47\) Inverses — Your Turn \(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x+8}{4}\) \(f^{-1}(x) = 2x-6\) \(f(g(x)) = 5\left(\dfrac{x-10}{5}\right)+10 = (x-10)+10=x\) ✓ No — \(f(2)=2\) and \(f(-2)=2\), so two different inputs give the same output, violating one-to-one. \(R^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{x-2}{3}\); at \(x=26\): \(R^{-1}(26)=\dfrac{24}{3}=8\) mg \(f^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{7-x}{2}\); \(f^{-1}(5)=1\), \(f(1)=-2+7=5\) ✓ Challenge — Harder Versions \((f\circ g)(x) = 2(x-4)+3 = 2x-5\); inverting: \(y=2x-5 \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{y+5}{2} \Rightarrow (f\circ g)^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{x+5}{2}\). (b) \(f^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{x-3}{2}\), \(g^{-1}(x)=x+4\); \((g^{-1}\circ f^{-1})(x) = \dfrac{x-3}{2}+4 = \dfrac{x-3+8}{2}=\dfrac{x+5}{2}\). Yes, both methods agree. Yes, one-to-one (each piece is individually increasing, and the pieces don’t overlap in output range in a way that causes repeats). Inverse: for \(x<0\) piece, \(y=x+3 \Rightarrow x=y-3\), valid for \(y<3\); for \(x\ge0\) piece, \(y=2x+3 \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{y-3}{2}\), valid for \(y\ge3\). So \(f^{-1}(x) = \begin{cases}x-3 & x<3\\ \frac{x-3}{2} & x\ge3\end{cases}\). Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications \(S(D(t)) = 40-5(0.15t) = 40-0.75t\). At \(t=20\): \(40-15=25\) minutes of estimated remaining survivable time. \(P(R(x)) = \dfrac{150x}{5000} = 0.03x\). Inverse: \(x = \dfrac{y}{0.03} \Rightarrow P^{-1}(y) = \dfrac{y}{0.03}\approx33.3y\). This tells a manager: to fund \(y\) patrol boats, they need a total allowable catch of about \(33.3y\) tons.

Two contaminants decay at different rates: Contaminant A starts at 100 units with a half-life of 4 years; Contaminant B starts at 40 units with a half-life of 12 years. Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from about 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to about 420 ppm today. Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Exponential Growth & Decay — Your Turn \(N(t)=500(1.15)^t\); \(N(6)=500(1.15)^6\approx500(2.313)\approx1156.7\) cells \(A(t)=200(0.5)^{t/10}\); \(A(30)=200(0.5)^3=200(0.125)=25\) units \(N(t)=3000(0.96)^t\); \(N(20)=3000(0.96)^{20}\approx3000(0.4420)\approx1326\) \(A(t)=80(0.5)^{t/6}\); \(A(18)=80(0.5)^3=80(0.125)=10\) units Growth, 2% per period Decay, 7% per period Logarithms — Your Turn \(\text{pH}=-\log_{10}(10^{-5})=5\) \([\text{H}^+]=10^{-3}\) mol/L \(2+1=3\) \(4-2=2\) Difference of 3 pH units \(\Rightarrow 10^3=1000\) times more acidic \(\log_2(64)=6\), since \(2^6=64\) Challenge — Harder Versions \(3(150)=150(1.06)^t \Rightarrow 3=(1.06)^t \Rightarrow t=\log_{1.06}(3)\approx18.85\) years. (Hint: solve \(s=r\theta\) for \(\theta\).) Notes: 19.4 Challenge 19.4.1 Harder Versions 1. A rational function is \(f(x) = \dfrac{ax+b}{x+c}\) with a known horizontal asymptote of 8 and a known vertical asymptote at \(x=-5\). If \(f(0) = 6\), find \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\). (Hint: the vertical asymptote tells you \(c\); the horizontal asymptote tells you \(a\); then use \(f(0)\) to find \(b\).) 2. Two circular regions have radii 40 m and 60 m, sharing the same center. Find the area of the ring (annulus) between them that corresponds to a \(90°\) wedge. (You’ll need the sector-area formula \(A=\frac12 r^2\theta\), applied to each circle, then subtracted.) Notes: 19.4.2 Environmental & Biological Applications 1. A drug’s blood concentration during continuous IV infusion approaches a steady-state level described by \(C(t) = \dfrac{50t+5}{t+2}\) (mg/L, \(t\) in hours). A GPS-tracked migratory bird’s flight path sweeps out an arc as it circles a thermal updraft with a radius of 200 m, completing three-quarters of a full circle before straightening out. Find the total arc length flown during that circling phase. (Hint: three-quarters of a circle is what angle, in radians?) Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Rational Functions & Equilibrium — Your Turn \(x=5\) \(x=-9\) \(4\) (ratio of leading coefficients: \(4x/x=4\)) \(5\) (ratio of leading coefficients: \(10x/2x=5\)) \(30\) (ratio of leading coefficients \(30t/t=30\)) \(C(0)=\dfrac{100}{20}=5\); \(C(20)=\dfrac{100+100}{40}=5\); horizontal asymptote \(=5\) (ratio \(5t/t=5\)). The concentration is constant at 5 for all \(t\) in this particular model — every value, including the asymptote, equals 5, so there’s no actual change over time here. Angular Measure & Arc Length — Your Turn \(\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\) \(150°\) \(s=25\times\dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{25\pi}{4}\approx19.6\) m \(40°=\dfrac{2\pi}{9}\) rad; \(s=10\times\dfrac{2\pi}{9}=\dfrac{20\pi}{9}\approx6.98\) km \(100°=\dfrac{5\pi}{9}\) rad; \(s=3\times\dfrac{5\pi}{9}=\dfrac{5\pi}{3}\approx5.24\) km \(\theta=\dfrac{s}{r}=\dfrac{2}{1.5}=1.\overline{3}\) rad \(\approx76.4°\) Challenge — Harder Versions Vertical asymptote at \(x=-5\) means \(c=5\) (since denominator is \(x+c=0 \Rightarrow x=-c\)). Horizontal asymptote of 8 means \(a=8\) (ratio of leading coefficients). Then \(f(0)=\dfrac{b}{c}=\dfrac{b}{5}=6 \Rightarrow b=30\). So \(f(x)=\dfrac{8x+30}{x+5}\). \(90°=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\) rad. Sector area (radius 60): \(\frac12(60)^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=900\pi\). Sector area (radius 40): \(\frac12(40)^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=400\pi\). Ring area: \(900\pi-400\pi=500\pi\approx1570.8\) m². Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications Steady-state (horizontal asymptote): ratio of leading coefficients \(=50\) mg/L. A rational function is a good fit because, unlike a linear function (which would grow forever) or an exponential function decaying to zero, this process approaches a nonzero constant — exactly the shape a horizontal asymptote captures. Three-quarters of a full circle \(=\dfrac34(2\pi)=\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\) radians. Arc length: \(s=200\times\dfrac{3\pi}{2}=300\pi\approx942.5\) m.

Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Circular Motion — Your Turn \(\omega=\dfrac{2\pi}{60}=\dfrac{\pi}{30}\) rad/s; \(v=15\times\dfrac{\pi}{30}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\approx1.57\) m/s \(\omega=\dfrac{2\pi}{5760}\approx0.00109\) rad/s; \(v=7000\times0.00109\approx7.63\) km/s \(\omega=1000\times2\pi=2000\pi\approx6283.2\) rad/min; \(v=12\times6283.2\approx75{,}398\) cm/min \(\omega=\dfrac{2\pi}{30}=\dfrac{\pi}{15}\); \(v=8\times\dfrac{\pi}{15}\approx1.68\) m/s \(\omega=\dfrac{2\pi}{4}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\) rad/s for both. \(v_{10}=10\times\dfrac{\pi}{2}\approx15.7\) m/s; \(v_{35}=35\times\dfrac{\pi}{2}\approx55.0\) m/s. The radius-35 point is faster, by a factor of exactly \(35/10=3.5\) (same as the ratio of radii, since \(\omega\) is the same for both). \(\omega=\dfrac{2\pi}{20}=\dfrac{\pi}{10}\); \(v=6\times\dfrac{\pi}{10}\approx1.88\) m/s Right-Triangle Trig & the Unit Circle — Your Turn \(\tan(\theta)=\dfrac{3}{20} \Rightarrow \theta=\tan^{-1}(0.15)\approx8.5°\) \(\tan(28°)=\dfrac{h}{50} \Rightarrow h=50\tan(28°)\approx26.6\) m \(\tan(\theta)=\dfrac{40}{60} \Rightarrow \theta=\tan^{-1}(0.667)\approx33.7°\) \(\sin(\pi/4)=\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\); \(\cos(\pi/6)=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\); \(\tan(\pi/4)=1\) \(\sin^2\theta=1-0.28^2=1-0.0784=0.9216 \Rightarrow \sin\theta\approx0.960\) \(\cos^2\theta=1-0.96^2=1-0.9216=0.0784 \Rightarrow \cos\theta\approx0.280\) Challenge — Harder Versions \(v=r\omega \le 80 \Rightarrow \omega \le \dfrac{80}{45}\approx1.778\) rad/s. Period \(T=\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}\), so minimum period: \(T\ge\dfrac{2\pi}{1.778}\approx3.53\) s. The blade must take at least about 3.53 seconds per rotation. Near observer: \(\tan(50°)=\dfrac{h}{x} \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{h}{\tan(50°)}\). Far observer: \(\tan(30°)=\dfrac{h}{x+40} \Rightarrow x+40=\dfrac{h}{\tan(30°)}\). Subtracting: \(\dfrac{h}{\tan(30°)}-\dfrac{h}{\tan(50°)}=40\). Solving: \(h\left(\dfrac{1}{0.5774}-\dfrac{1}{1.1918}\right)=40 \Rightarrow h(1.732-0.839)=40 \Rightarrow h(0.893)=40 \Rightarrow h\approx44.8\) m. Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications \(\omega=\dfrac{2\pi}{8}=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) rad/s; \(v=50\times\dfrac{\pi}{4}\approx39.3\) m/s (horizontal speed). This calculation only accounts for motion around the circle (horizontal); it ignores the bird’s simultaneous vertical climb, so its true total speed through the air is somewhat higher than this horizontal-only figure. \(h=25\tan(38°)\approx25(0.781)\approx19.5\) m. A source of error: a small error in the measured 25 m ground distance (e.g. due to uneven terrain or imprecise pacing) propagates directly into the height estimate, since height is proportional to that distance for a fixed angle.

Inverse Trig & Solving for Time — Your Turn \(\sin^{-1}(0)=0\); \(\cos^{-1}(1)=0\); \(\sin^{-1}(-1)=-\pi/2\) \(12-10=4(1) \Rightarrow \sin(t)=0.5 \Rightarrow t=\pi/6\)… check: \(4\sin(\pi/6)+10=4(0.5)+10=12\) ✓, so \(t=\pi/6\) \(3.0-2.5=1.5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) \Rightarrow \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right)=\dfrac13 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}t=\sin^{-1}(0.333)\approx0.3398 \Rightarrow t\approx0.649\) hr \(20-15=10\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3)\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3)=\pi/6 \Rightarrow t-3=1 \Rightarrow t=4\) Yes — sine is symmetric about its peak, so there is a second month where the curve crosses the same value on its way back down, mirrored around the month where the maximum occurs. \(8-7=2\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}t\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{12}t=\pi/6 \Rightarrow t=2\) hr Challenge — Harder Versions \(A=9-4=5\). \(B=\dfrac{2\pi}{10}=\dfrac{\pi}{5}\). Max at \(t=5\) means midline-crossing (rising) at \(t=5-\dfrac{10}{4}=2.5\), so \(C=2.5\). Equation: \(f(t)=5\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{5}(t-2.5)\right)+4\). From the worked demo/practice: one solution near \(t\approx0.649\) hr. Using the period (\(2\pi/(\pi/6)=12\) hr) and sine’s symmetry: second solution in cycle 1 at \(t=6-0.649=5.351\) hr. The phase shift of 4 means the herd is at its average elevation (2000 m) and moving upward starting around month 4. \(15-10=8\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{12}(t-6)\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.625 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-6)=\sin^{-1}(0.625)\approx0.6751 \Rightarrow t-6\approx1.29 \Rightarrow t\approx7.29\), so around month 7 the toxin level first reaches the closure threshold.

A conservation survey is only funded for months when the population is predicted to exceed 1500. Find the approximate range of months this covers, and briefly explain what limitation this single idealized model might have for actually planning real survey timing (connecting back to the group discussion in the capstone activity above). Notes: Answer Key Click to reveal Your Turn and Challenge answers Solving Sinusoidal Equations — Your Turn Second solution: \(t=\pi-\pi/6=5\pi/6\) \(3.6-3=1.2\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}t=\pi/6 \Rightarrow t=1\). Second solution: \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}t=\pi-\pi/6=5\pi/6 \Rightarrow t=5\). Both: \(t=1\) and \(t=5\) hours. \(22-18=8\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-2)\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-2)=\pi/6 \Rightarrow t-2=1 \Rightarrow t=3\). Second solution: \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-2)=5\pi/6 \Rightarrow t-2=5 \Rightarrow t=7\). Both: month 3 and month 7. Full Modeling Workflow — Capstone (sample solution) A repeating annual cycle with a single high and low point strongly suggests a sinusoidal function. \(D=\dfrac{1.8+0.4}{2}=1.1\); \(A=\dfrac{1.8-0.4}{2}=0.7\) Period \(=12\) months; \(B=\dfrac{2\pi}{12}=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\) Peak at month 4 means midline-crossing (rising) at month 1, so \(C=1\) \(L(t)=0.7\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-1)\right)+1.1\) \(L(7)=0.7\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(6)\right)+1.1=0.7\sin(\pi)+1.1=0.7(0)+1.1=1.1\) m Sample discussion points: rainfall variability from year to year, upstream water management decisions, drought years, or an unusually warm/cold season could all shift the real data away from a perfectly smooth idealized cycle. Challenge — Harder Versions \(A=6\), \(D=10\), \(B=\dfrac{2\pi}{8}=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\). A minimum occurs a quarter-period after a downward midline crossing; equivalently, a minimum at \(t=3\) means the maximum occurs half a period earlier or later, i.e. at \(t=3-4=-1\) or \(t=3+4=7\). The maximum corresponds to an upward midline-crossing a quarter period before it, so using the max at \(t=7\): upward crossing at \(t=7-2=5\), giving \(C=5\). Equation: \(f(t)=6\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}(t-5)\right)+10\). (Check: at \(t=3\), \(\frac{\pi}{4}(3-5)=-\frac{\pi}{2}\), \(\sin(-\pi/2)=-1\), giving \(f(3)=6(-1)+10=4\), the minimum — confirmed.) \(8-6=4\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}t\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{4}t=\pi/6 \Rightarrow t=2/3\). Second solution in first cycle: \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}t=5\pi/6 \Rightarrow t=10/3\). Period \(=\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/4}=8\), so repeating: \(t=2/3+8=26/3\) and \(t=10/3+8=34/3\). All four solutions in \(0\) to \(16\): \(t\approx0.67, 3.33, 8.67, 11.33\). Challenge — Environmental & Biological Applications \(16-12=6\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-5)\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.667 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-5)\approx0.7297 \Rightarrow t-5\approx1.39 \Rightarrow t\approx6.39\). Second crossing: \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-5)\approx\pi-0.7297\approx2.412 \Rightarrow t-5\approx4.61 \Rightarrow t\approx9.61\). Elevated risk approximately from month 6.4 to month 9.6. \(1500-1200=800\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{12}(t-3)\right) \Rightarrow \sin(\cdot)=0.375 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3)\approx0.3844 \Rightarrow t-3\approx0.73 \Rightarrow t\approx3.73\). Second crossing: \(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3)\approx\pi-0.3844\approx2.757 \Rightarrow t-3\approx5.27 \Rightarrow t\approx8.27\). Funded survey window: roughly month 3.7 to month 8.3. Limitation: real migration timing shifts year to year with weather and food availability, so a fixed idealized funding window based on one averaged model may miss an early or late migration in an unusual year.

Precalculus Equation Sheet Lines & Circles Concept Form Slope-intercept \(y = mx + b\) Point-slope \(y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)\) Circle \((x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2\) Distance formula \(d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}\) Quadratics Concept Form Standard form \(f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c\) Vertex form \(f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k\) Vertex (from standard form) \(x = -\dfrac{b}{2a}\) Exponential & Logarithmic Concept Form Exponential growth/decay \(f(t) = a \cdot b^t\) Logarithm definition \(\log_b(x) = y \iff b^y = x\) Product rule \(\log_b(xy) = \log_b(x) + \log_b(y)\) Quotient rule \(\log_b(x/y) = \log_b(x) - \log_b(y)\) Power rule \(\log_b(x^n) = n\log_b(x)\) Rational Functions & Angular Measure Concept Form Degrees to radians \(\text{radians} = \text{degrees} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}\) Arc length \(s = r\theta\) Trigonometry & Sinusoidal Functions Concept Form Sinusoidal function \(f(t) = A\sin\big(B(t-C)\big) + D\) Amplitude \(A\) Period \(\dfrac{2\pi}{B}\) Phase shift \(C\) Vertical shift \(D\) Pythagorean identity \(\sin^2(\theta) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1\)
